question: What does @Cacheable do when the same method is called with the same arguments a second time?
options: Runs the method again and overwrites the cached value
Throws a CacheHitException to signal the cache was used
Skips the method body entirely and returns the previously cached result
Runs the method and compares the result to the cached value for consistency

question: What is the key difference between @Cacheable and @CachePut?
options: @Cacheable is for reads; @CachePut is for deletes
@Cacheable skips method execution on a cache hit; @CachePut always runs the method and updates the cache
@CachePut requires a Redis backend; @Cacheable works with any provider
@Cacheable works with Spring Data; @CachePut only works with plain repositories

question: A product update saves to the database but findById() still returns the old product. What is the most likely cause?
options: The database transaction was not committed
@CacheEvict or @CachePut was not added to the update method — the cache was never invalidated
The cache provider does not support updates
@Cacheable requires readOnly = true to detect changes

question: Which cache provider is appropriate for a multi-instance production deployment where all instances must share the same cache?
options: Simple (ConcurrentHashMap) — it is the fastest option
Caffeine — because it supports replication between JVM instances
Redis — a shared, external cache that all instances connect to
EhCache — stores cache on local disk and syncs between servers

question: You want to cache the result of getUserById(Long userId) but only if the user's account is active. Which annotation attribute enables this?
options: key = "#result.active"
unless = "#result.active == false"
condition = "#result != null && #result.active"
sync = true
